the Municipal Accountant

documents of the
transactions included in
the Accounts Payable
account and inform the
suppliers to present
their corresponding
claims duly supported
with the required
documents for
processing and payment
thereof;
b) Return/remit to the
source agency any
unused/unexpended
balance if so stipulated
in the MOA/Trust
Agreement in order to
reflect the correct
balance of the account
in the financial
statements;
c) Make representation
with all the suppliers
and contractors by
preparing letters
informing them of the
amount due them and
the required supporting
documents to the
claims. The GSO and
the Municipal Engineer
may provide assistance
in securing the required
documents. After
which, assess or
evaluate the unclaimed
retention money and
consider reversion to
the unappropriated
surplus of any
unclaimed amount;
d) Prepare a listing of the
liability accounts to be
considered as
miscellaneous income
which will be closed to
Government Equity and
could be appropriated
for other priority
projects instead of
being kept unattended
or left idle.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant submits the depreciation/ lapsing schedules for CY 2023 for further examination by the Audit Team, including a written explanation on the non-submission of such.

The Municipal Treasurer and the Supply Officer create and maintain a complete monitoring of all inventories through Stock Cards, in hard copy, and ensure proper and consistent assignment of stock numbers for every inventory item.

The Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer conduct reconciliation on the noted differences in the monthly expenses and balances of all inventory accounts monitored, submit a written explanation thereof, and make adjustments in the books or in the supply records, when necessary.

The Municipal Mayor transfers management of inventories from other departments to the Municipal Treasurer and the Supply Officer for proper issuance of documents relating to receipts and issuances and proper monitoring of balances; and

The Municipal Accountant submits a written explanation on the various deficiencies noted on various account entries and balances, conduct tracing and draw JEVs, if necessary, to adjust the balances of these accounts.

The Municipal Accountant submits a written explanation on the noted difference of ₱495.00 between the trial balance and the Notes to Financial Statements of the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account, including the noted difference between the CY 2023 LDRRMF unspent balance and the Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds account balance by ₱8,055,324.16.

The Municipal Treasurer causes the transfer of the total unexpended balance of the LDRRMF totaling ₱8,547,070.44 to the Trust Fund; and

The Municipal Treasurer submits a written explanation on the noted difference between the Trust Liabilities - DRRMF and the ending balance of the trust liability bank account of ₱20,273,211.22.

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer trace and analyze all transactions recorded in each of the aforementioned records as far as practicable to identify the source of the noted differences; and



The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer initiate steps towards the proper recording of sources of the noted differences in the appropriate records.

The Municipal Accountant conducts tracing of current and prior years transactions of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds of all funds and take note of the necessary adjustments; and

The Municipal Accountant prepares the necessary JEVs with the end in view of reflecting the reconciled balances of the intra-agency accounts of all funds as of December 31, 2023.

The Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer conduct tracing, validation and inspection to determine whether PPE declared as unserviceable totaling ₱1,568,591.00 exists and valid for disposal to immediately initiate disposal thereof; otherwise, restate such items as PPE in the books.
The Supply Officer initiate steps towards the proper disposal of these properties in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.

The Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials/ employees expedite the completion of the necessary documents needed for the implementation of pending development projects as of December 31, 2023 with the end in view of completing all of these projects prior to the end of CY 2024 to the benefit of the constituents.

The Municipal Mayor submit a written explanation on the utilization of ₱21,229,470.29 without specific sanggunian approval, including submission of additional supporting documents for further evaluation thereof.

The Municipal Budget Officer submit authority on the excess utilization totaling ₱395,221.17, including a written explanation on the incurrence of such deficiencies, for further evaluation thereof.
The GFPS submit to the Audit Team a revised and duly approved CY 2023 GAD AR for further evaluation.

The GFPS provides technical assistance to the MPDC towards the maintenance of a complete GAD database in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended, and ensure that the database is complete and sex-disaggregated in accordance with the data requirements as presented in Annex B of the JMC.

The Municipal Mayor issue an EO establishing a gender-responsive M&E system through creating the M&E Team in compliance with Item 5.0 of PCW-DBM-DILB-NEDA JMC 2013-01, as amended.

The Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials initiate steps towards the revision of the approved plantilla of personnel taking into consideration the minimum organizational structure and staffing pattern of the Office of the Municipal Assessor in compliance with Section 472 of RA No. 7160 and Chapter I, Section 5 of the Manual on Real Property Appraisal and Assessment Operations; and 

The Municipal Mayor directs the HRMO to initiate steps towards the hiring, reassignment and transfer of personnel to the Office of the Municipal Assessor with the end in view of filling-in the minimum organizational structure and staffing pattern.

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer identify the debtors of these receivable balances for proper issuance of demand to collect such with the end in view of closing these balances and prevent them from being dormant; and

1.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all disbursements are accompanied with proper
documentation. Also, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to require the
issuance of SIs/ORs from the suppliers/contractors for the goods delivered and
services rendered aforementioned above and submit the same to the Municipal
Accountant for the latter to immediately submit to the Audit Team in order to
avoid audit suspension.

2.4 We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit to the Bureau of Treasury or
return to the SAs savings from implementation of projects in order to fully
liquidate these accounts and to end any obligation created from these
transfers.

3.4 We recommended that Management stop the practice of paying in advance
for goods or services not yet delivered or rendered and strictly adhere to the
provisions of section 88 of PD 1445 in order to prevent possible loss of
public funds. Moreover, instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer not to approve and release payment for such transactions in order
to prevent the risk of non-performance of such service and the possible non-
recovery of the advance payment made.

4.5 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer,
in coordination with the Accounting Department, ensure full compliance
with Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 by requiring the submission
and attachment of complete supporting documents—including Inspection
Reports and Statements of Time Elapsed—for all progress payments in order
to substantiate the payment made and strengthen the internal control over
completion projects and its corresponding payments.

5.8 We recommended that the LCE:
a. Direct the LSB to strictly comply with the allowable uses of the SEF as
outlined in Section 272 of R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint
Circular No. 1, s. 2017. Charge administrative expenses against the SEF
only if such is directly related to and essential for the implementation of
priority educational programs and projects.
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to refrain
from processing or disbursing SEF allocations for travelling expenses
relating to seminars/trainings in order to avoid potential disallowances
during audit and to ensure that SEF resources are used solely for their
intended purpose.

6.9 We recommended that the concerned officials of the LSB and the Schools
Division Office strictly comply with the above-cited provisions, by ensuring
that all appropriated capital outlay funds in the annual school board budget
are properly itemized into specific PPAs, along with their corresponding
costs. This will facilitate better planning, timely implementation and
monitoring of the priority programs and projects in public schools within the
municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed, through the Inventory Committee, to ensure the conduct of complete physical count of PPE and Investment Property and submit a complete RPCPPE.
We also recommended and the Supply Officer agreed to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant towards assignment of unit values for the Land and Building based on actual costs.
Lastly, we also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Initiate steps to ensure that property and accounting records are fully reconciled; and
Revise the Notes to the Financial Statements taking into consideration the additional disclosure on local road networks as required in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer agreed to conduct tracing, validation and inspection to determine whether PPEs declared as unserviceable totaling ₱3,231,753.53 exist and qualify for disposal and to immediately initiate disposal thereof; otherwise, restate such items as PPE in the books.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to set-up the allowance for impairment on the unserviceable properties with carrying amounts totaling ₱319,445.74, consistent with the requirements of IPSAS No. 21 to present fairly the PPE accounts in the financial statements.
We further recommended and the Supply Officer agreed to prepare the IIRUP and IIRUSP as separate reports, and ensure that all required information for each property, such as acquisition date and unit cost, are accurately determined and documented for proper recording and accountability.
Moreover, we recommended and Management agreed, that the Disposal Committee:
Evaluate if there is a need for adjustments of appraised values, in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties; and
Expedite the disposal proceedings of the unserviceable properties.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Inventory Committee, to conduct a complete physical count of inventory of supplies for semestral cutoffs June 30 and December 31 of every year and render the RPCI, which shall be submitted to the Audit Team in the form and deadline as required in Section 124 of the Manual.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer agreed to immediately conduct tracing, validation, and reconciliation whenever discrepancies are identified following the physical inventory count of supplies.

We recommended and the Management agreed, through the Municipal Information System Office, to determine the applicable useful life of the acquired Computer Software.
After determination of the useful life, we further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Compute the amortization in accordance with IPSAS No. 31 and its PAG;
Record the amortization expense of the Computer Software in accordance with IPSAS No. 31.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Municipal Accounting Office, to conduct a regular reconciliation and tracing of the current and prior year Due from and Due to Other Funds accounts and transactions of all funds to identify the cause/s of the discrepancy and to effect the necessary adjustments to bring the balances of the accounts into agreement.

We recommended and Management agreed to establish efficient internal controls over financial accounting and reporting of the Municipality, such as timely submission of liquidation reports and other supporting documents to the Accounting Office in order to facilitate timely recording and reporting and to prevent the suspension of payment of salaries of the responsible officials/employees causing delay thereon pursuant to Section 122(2) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to consistently and timely submit the DVs and its supporting documents within the deadlines, for proper accountability and to prevent the suspension of payment of salaries of the officials concerned in cases of failure to submit the documents and reports under Section 122(2) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the monthly BRS per bank account to properly identify the reconciling items; and
Draw adjusting/correcting entries for valid reconciling items to ensure the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank and other affected accounts in the financial statements.
The management commented that if allowed, they will submit monthly the soft copies first and the hardcopies to follow.
The Team understands and allows softcopies to be submitted due to budget constraints for TEV, but it should be in complete set like, the inclusion of bank statements & cashbook for the month.

We recommended the following and:
The Municipal Treasurer agreed to summarize the collections according to the type of income such as business taxes, licenses, service income, rentals from market operations, community tax certificates, etc. through an Abstract of Collections and Deposits and not a generic description as “general collections”.
The Municipal Accountant agreed to record the collections based on the summary made by the Municipal Treasurer in the Report on Collections and Deposits using the appropriate account titles and descriptions provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009.

The Municipal Accountant agreed to set up separate subsidiary ledgers for the trust liabilities depending on the nature of the collections such as bid documents as well as the 10% share for the arresting officer from the fines from the illegal fishing.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to consider preparing the following proposed adjusting journal entries to correct the erroneous entries made:
Transfers to various barangays totaling ₱2,040,000.00 granted for specific purpose were recorded as ‘Subsidy to other LGUs’ instead of ‘Due from LGUs’ inconsistent with the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, resulting in overstatement of the expense, understatement of the receivable and the non-monitoring of the subsequent utilization of funds.
Our review of the following transactions for CY 2024 showed that the Municipality granted various financial assistance totaling ₱2,040,000.00 charged to the 20% Development fund as part of their ₱8 million appropriation for Various Development Projects for barangays in the CY 2024 Annual Investment Program (AIP).
Our further verification disclosed that these grants were all recorded as an outright expense under ‘Subsidy to Other LGUs’ account instead of having it recorded as a receivable under ‘Due from LGUs’ account in accordance with the provisions of the above-mentioned 2015 circular.
Moreover, these fund transfers were not supported with corresponding Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) that would define the duties and responsibilities of the source and implementing government agencies pursuant to COA Circular No. 1994-013. This circular provides that the Source Agency shall enter into an agreement with the Implementing Agency for the undertaking by the latter of the project of the former.  The agreement shall provide for the requirements for project implementation and reporting.
These transfers funded from the 20% DF were granted to fund specific programs/projects/activities. Thus, these should be properly recorded and monitored in separate subsidiary ledgers in order to establish proper accountability, as well as transparency in the liquidation and utilization of these fund transfers.
The Municipal Accounting Office explained that these were recorded as an outright expense especially those under MOOE.  Nonetheless, it has been agreed upon that the barangays submit a liquidation report for each type of financial assistance received from the Municipality.
The erroneous treatment of financial assistance granted to barangays resulted in the overstatement and understatement of expense and receivable accounts, respectively.  It also resulted in the non-preparation of separate subsidiary ledgers for each transfer for monitoring of its subsequent liquidation including the determination whether the funds were spent in accordance with its specific purpose. Further, the responsibilities of the source and implementing agencies were not clearly defined due to the absence of corresponding MOA for each transfer.
We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant to evaluate those transfers/ financial assistance to other LGUs with specific purposes and determine if Fund Utilization Reports were already submitted by the recipient LGUs, otherwise, require their submission prior to the release of subsequent financial assistance;
For unliquidated fund transfers, the Accounting Unit to effect the necessary adjusting entries as follows:
The Municipal Accountant to carefully review the transactions during the month and prepare subsidiary ledgers for each transfer with specific purpose for proper monitoring of its utilization; and
Execute a MOA between the source (municipality) and the implementing (recipient) barangays for every grant of financial assistance with specific purpose to properly define their respective responsibilities
The Management agreed to comply with the recommendations. However, regarding the execution of a MOA for financial grants to barangays, the management asked if the MOA for sociocultural financial assistance of ₱10,000.00 or less could be waived because such an amount is too small and all barangays receive such subsidies annually.
The team informed the management that once the adjusting entries were completed, they shall submit a copy of the JEV to support the adjustment entries made.
For financial assistance of ₱10,000.00 or less, the team suggested that they could develop a general guideline that would identify the source agency, the obligations of the implementing agency, and the deadline for the implementing agency to liquidate the subsidy.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the transactions using the appropriate accounts pursuant to the descriptions provided for in COA Circular Nos. 2015-009 and 2024-006.

We recommended and Management agreed through the Municipal Accountant, to prioritize compliance with COA Circular 2024-006 and consider preparing the following proposed adjusting journal entries to correct the affected asset and expense accounts:
We further recommended and Management agreed through the General Services Officer, to issue to the end-users of the semi-expendable property the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS) to establish accountability over such item.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the financial transactions using the description provided for in COA Circular No. 2015-009 and prepare the necessary adjusting entry as proposed below.

We recommended and Management:
Through the Municipal Treasurer, agreed to deposit immediately the amount not yet deposited and henceforth, deposit intact the collections pursuant to the LTOM and MNGAS for LGUs; and
Through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to issue demand letters to the previous Accountable Officer for the undeposited collections in the SEF and apply the said amount from the claims of the subject AO, if there is any.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Discontinue the practice of releasing cash advances in excess of the three-month requirement of the duration of the implementation of the projects and strict compliance with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01; and
Re-evaluate, in coordination with the Chief of Police, whether there is still a need for the allocation of Confidential Fund. Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs provides that RPT/SET Receivables shall be established at the beginning of the year based on Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR)/Taxpayer’s index card. At the beginning of the year, the Treasurer shall furnish the Chief Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year. Based on the list, the Chief Accountant shall draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and crediting to Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.

No. 1445; Investigate/trace the unreconciled differences, take appropriate actions by providing adjusting entries and monitor these accounts in order to prevent further discrepancies. The Municipal Accountant explained that the granting of cash advances on confidential funds on a semestral basis was mainly due to their practice of submission of liquidation documents to ICFAU through mail.  The receipt of the duly received copy of the transmittal from ICFAU would take three weeks to one month; hence, the Special Disbursing Officer could not be granted timely cash advance on a quarterly basis.  Nonetheless, the Municipal Accountant assured to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to submit the disbursement vouchers and its attachments on the granting of cash advances for CF within seven working days from release of the check pursuant to COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials:
Assess the manpower needs of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office taking into consideration their actual work performed as against their official designation in compliance with DBM LBC No. 137 pertaining to position titles in local government units; and
In the event these casual employees continued performing revenue collection functions, direct the Municipal Treasurer to secure their fidelity bonds pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 2-2019.
The Municipal Treasurer asserted that the Municipality had taken the initiative to align these Municipal employees' position titles.  Additionally, some of these personnel were already promoted as Revenue Collectors Clerks while others were designated as Revenue Collectors.  Nonetheless, all of them were properly bonded.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to ensure that all unissued Accountable Forms under the custody of their Office be kept in a locked fireproof/waterproof cabinet or safe.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials conduct an extensive and holistic planning both during project conceptualization and implementation to ensure timely completion of identified development projects.
The Municipal Mayor together with the concerned officials assured that the following priority development projects will be implemented in CY 2024:
Senior Citizen’s Park (Phase II)
Gato Island Site Development and Multi-Purpose Building for Bantay Dagat Outpost
Construction of Multi-Purpose Building / Philippine Coastguard Sub-station at Carnaza Island
Meat Van (Municipal Agriculturist / Slaughterhouse)
Improvement of Public Market Comfort Rooms
Tapilon and Poblacion Road Signages
Purchase & Development of Sanitary Landfill
On the other hand, the Management explained that the projects “Bitoon Municipal Cemetery Improvement” and “Purchase of Lot (Cemetery) at Barangay Tapilon” were already realigned to other priority development projects in CY 2024 while the following projects were confirmed to have not yet been implemented:
Electrification of Recovery Containment Area at Barangay Pajo, Daanbantayan
Horizontal Development and Fencing New Sports Complex
Design of Facilities and Supply and Installation of Waste Materials Equipment (MENRO)

The Municipal Mayor confirmed that the Municipality had lump-sum appropriations in the 20 per cent DF primarily on their financial assistance/aid to barangays.  Such appropriations were intended to accommodate the priority development projects of the Barangays which the details of the projects were yet to be identified during the Budgeting process.
The Audit Team reiterates that the subsequent utilization of these lump-sum appropriations requires a covering contract for each specific priority development project that requires approval by the Sanggunian, thus delaying its implementation.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
Submit a written explanation/justification on the charging of the cost of repairs/improvement of the Municipal Hall Building Structure against the 20 per cent DF contrary to Item 3.2.5 of the JMC; and
Require the Municipal officials concerned to strictly comply with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2020-1 that provides for the proper charging of expenditure items on the 20 per cent DF in order to maximize the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to:
Require performance security on these infrastructure projects from the contractor with validity period until the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance, which is one year after the project completion, if still necessary; and
In coordination with the Municipal Accountant cause the submission of performance securities for the implementation of infrastructure projects from the contractors with validity periods in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 IRR of R.A. No. 9184

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the application of the 10 per cent retention money on all progress payments to contractors on the implementation of infrastructure projects or request substitution of retention with irrevocable standby letters of credit or from a commercial bank, bank guarantees or surety bonds callable on demand in accordance with Item 6 of Annex E of the 2016 IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

The Municipal Mayor and the concerned Municipal officials commented that the following projects were subject for realignment to other projects and already awaiting replies from the source agencies:
Carnaza Barangay Health Station
Daanbantayan Central School Faculty Room
Reconstruction of Slaughterhouse – Yolanda Rehabilitation and Reconstruction Program (YRRP)
Rehabilitation of Greenhouse
Rehabilitation of Nursery
Rehabilitation of Seeds Bodega
On the other hand, the Management asserted that the implementation of the “DOLE Integrated Livelihood and Emergency Employment Program (DILEEP)” project is still in on-going status in CY 2024 while the project “Support for the Operation of Materials Recovery Facility (purchase of extruder machine)” was yet to be implemented.

The Municipal Accountant asserted that the unexpended balances on various financial assistance from Provincial Government of Cebu will be refunded and fully liquidated in CY 2024.  Moreover, the project “Concrete Tables & Chairs at Kandaya Sunset Boulevard” is to be implemented in CY 2024 while the project “Swine fever surveillance and organic pig production” is yet to be implemented as the Municipality is still awaiting guidelines for its utilization.
The Management further explained that the amount of ₱3,000,000.00 financial assistance for various projects was subject for realignment and awaiting approval from the Province of Cebu.  Also, the financial assistance received from the City Government of Davao, City Government of Naga and City Government of Talisay were already utilized in CY 2024.

We recommended and the:
Municipal Mayor agreed in coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan to exercise the prerogative and give priority to the revision and updating of the 2011 Local Revenue Code pursuant to the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 to help meet the financial needs of the Municipality; and
Municipal Treasurer and all other concerned department heads agreed to ensure that the revised rate of fees and charges are just and reasonable taking into consideration the application of LFC Toolkit pursuant to DBM-DOF JMC No. 2019-01.

We reiterated our recommendation and the GAD Focal Person agreed to seriously consider the conduct of capability building of its proper officials and employees in the use of the HGDG in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01, as amended under MC 2016-01 so as to address this recurring audit observation.

We recommended that the MCPC prepare and submit to the Audit Team the CY 2023 year-end accomplishment report as indicated in the SOMA and the State of Children Report duly received by the Provincial Inter-Agency Monitoring Task Force.
The Management asserted that the Municipality prepared its CY 2023 accomplishment report which were incorporated in the SOMA and the State of Children Report.  These were duly received by the DILG.
The Audit Team requested that a copy of the said CY 2023 Accomplishment Report be submitted for further evaluation.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
Provide explanation as to the payment of consultancy fees on reported accomplishments beyond the terms of reference as indicated in the Contract of Services of the Legal Consultant on Barangay Affairs;
Determine the specific tasks and duties of these consultants including the scope of their work with an appropriate work program/schedule so as to ensure that the reported accomplishments of these consultants are aligned with that of the work program/schedule; and
Review and approve these reported accomplishments of the hired consultants in order to determine whether such accomplishments are in accordance with their respective Contract of Services and prevent any future controversies that may arise on the veracity of the consultant’s accomplishment reports.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Withhold the necessary income tax on professionals for payments to these consultants pursuant to BIR Revenue Regulations No. 11-2018; and
Ensure that payments to consultancy services are supported with complete documentation taking into consideration the attachment of their respective duly approved accomplishment reports.

We recommended and Management agreed to immediately correct the noted deficiencies and comply as well with the Notice to Correct Violations of the Bureau of Fire to ensure the safety of the occupants of the buildings owned and so that no fines, penalties, abatements or closures are imposed on the agency.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to initiate steps to insure all insurable properties of the Municipality in the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS through compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-001.

We recommended that Management stop giving allowances to observers on their attendance to pre-bid conference and public bidding unless Management could provide sufficient legal basis to support such provision of allowances.
The BAC Chairperson explained that allowances/honoraria were provided to observers in order to encourage their attendance in the procurement activities in order to promote transparency.
The Audit Team reiterates that the provision of allowances/honoraria is bereft of legal basis.  As such, the attendance of observers in procurement activities is only voluntary.  Nonetheless, the Municipality is required to invite observers for its procurement activities as provided in R.A. 9184.  The Audit Team also further emphasizes that the Municipality comply with the provisions of R.A. 9184 particularly on the qualifications of the observers based on the procurement type.

observations.recommendation: Management, through the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Offices, reconcile their records and adjust accordingly the RPT and SET balances in accordance with the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

Management, through the BAC and the Engineering Office, submit the noted lacking documentary requirements, which were communicated through clarificatory letters sent on various dates, for evaluation by the Technical Audit Group of the Commission.

BAC institute remedial measures in its organizational structure as warranted to ensure that the detailed engineering is complete, sufficient and correct before undertaking any procurement activities.

Evaluate the performance of the JOs assigned in various offices and determine if some of them would be qualified to be hired as regular employees or elevated to casual status; and

Prepare and submit the duly signed contract or memorandum of agreement between the Municipality and the JOs to define the duties and responsibilities and the output to be accomplished.

Management reevaluate its existing cash requirements and henceforth, ensure compliance with the provisions of the fidelity bond requirement either by increasing the amount of bond of its disbursing officer or by designating another DO to be accountable for the excess accountability of the current DO under a separate fidelity bonding requirement as deemed appropriate and consistently accomplish the month-end certifications in the cashbook.

Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the Subsidiary Ledgers for unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer.

Direct the Municipal Accountant, after ascertaining the details of Receivables and assessment of the collectability, set-up the allowance for impairment to present fairly the Loans Receivable-Others and Due from LGU accounting amounting to ₱11,888,098.02 and ₱109,543.59, respectively, in the financial statements at their net realizable amounts consistent with the requirements of IPSAS 29.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the municipal officials and employees exert effort to collect the long outstanding receivables.  If collection cannot be undertaken due to insufficiency of records or if despite employing all the possible and necessary collection procedures and these remain uncollected, the Municipality should request for the writing off of the receivables following these procedures:

a.The Municipal Accountant should make recommendations addressed to the Honorable Mayor who, in turn shall forward the request for writing off to the Sangguniang Bayan for the adoption of a resolution.  The request should be supported with documents evidencing the action/s taken/effort exerted to establish the validity and existence of accounts and to collect the receivables; and


b.The request together with the SB Resolution shall be forwarded by the Local  Chief Executive to the Commission on Audit for review and for approval.

observations.recommendation:
For the foregoing infractions noted, we have recommended the following:

a.The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) should adopt in its procurement  appropriate procedures taking into consideration that determination of the most advantageous bid differ for the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects from consulting services.


b.The Technical Working Group (TWG) should fully observe and comply  the requirements of Section 34.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the post-qualification to ensure that only bidders with the lowest calculated responsive bids are awarded the contracts.

c.The BAC should monitor from time to time new issuances/resolutions of the GPPB and effect changes in the procurement process/es where necessary to be able to comply fully the requirements of bidding.

d.The BAC should strictly observe the procedures and follow the criteria under Alternative Modes of Procurement particularly in the use of Negotiated Procurement – Two-Failed Biddings where posting of the  Invitation to Bid must be made twice. 

e.The Municipality’s use of Small Value Procurement (SMV) should strictly be in accordance with Section 52.1 of RA 9184.
